narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 45.5% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, LSAT score of 32, Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles both at 2, and family income in the high bracket, among other details like your full-time enrollment plans. The model weighed these factors against patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on those most predictive of bar success. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 grade decile of 2, which is notably lower than the average of about 6 for students who passed the bar, signaling to the model a potential challenge in sustaining strong performance through later undergrad years. Counterbalancing that, your undergraduate GPA of 3.50 stood out positively—it’s higher than the 3.24 average among bar passers—pulling the prediction toward success and nearly offsetting the Year 3 concern. Your LSAT score of 32 also nudged things in a favorable direction, though less powerfully, as it was somewhat below the typical 37 seen in successful students but still contributed to showing potential. While your high family income aligns with a smaller group of bar passers (only about 8.5%), it wasn’t among the top drivers here. Overall, your profile compares to accepted students by having a solid GPA edge but falling short in decile rankings and LSAT, placing you in a riskier category for first-time bar passage. This isn’t a judgment on your effort or background—it’s the model highlighting where patterns suggest hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, and if possible, strengthen your recent academic decile through additional coursework or grad-level performance. You might also connect with advisors for bar prep strategies tailored to students with similar starting points, as many have turned around predictions like yours. With targeted improvements, a future application could look much stronger to the model. Keep pushing—you’ve already shown GPA strength that others envy.
